verdict: 2026-07-12 20:04 PDT — The server deletion cascade is structurally clean: appContext/resolveUid has no runtime consumer outside the removable middleware, the store uses only Node built-ins, and OAuth, health, logging, security headers, and static serving are isolated and can remain intact. The design is not ready for a security/marketplace-sensitive teardown, however, because its proposed privacy claim is broader than the remaining data flows, its reconciliation inventory is incomplete, and it knowingly leaves a stated marketplace obligation unresolved.

findings.title: “No presenter data stored server-side” overclaims the remaining system
findings.claim: Removing the rate store does not make the server data-free. `/api/log` deliberately writes client diagnostics and error text to Railway logs, request logging remains, and OAuth temporarily processes authorization codes and tokens. The current privacy policy correctly treats operational logs separately and the retention policy says Railway retains them. Replacing that with an unqualified “no presenter data is stored server-side” claim—and broadly dropping export/delete-rights language—could misstate collection, retention, and data-subject handling even though presenter configuration is no longer persisted.
findings.alternative: Use the narrower invariant everywhere: “No presenter configuration, rates, names, or meeting history are persisted by the application; those values are session-only.” Preserve and reconcile the operational-logging disclosure, its retention/access rules, OAuth processing disclosure, privacy contact, and a process for applicable access/deletion requests. Before ratification, inventory the actual fields emitted to `/api/log` and Railway’s request/platform metadata.
findings.win: Eliminates a false public legal claim while retaining accurate disclosures and a rights-request path for the server-side data that still exists.

findings.title: The claims-reconciliation file list misses live contradictory policies and docs
findings.claim: The sketch says it will reconcile all claims but names only three policy files. Actual contradictory claims remain in `dev-docs/policies/data-retention-and-protection.md`, `dev-docs/policies/dependency-management.md`, and `dev-docs/opportunity-cost-rate.md`; `server/src/zoom/oauth.js` also says App Context verifies requests. These files are outside the scope-containment list, so the prescribed final grep would either fail or pressure the implementation to exceed its own allowed file list.
findings.alternative: Build the rewrite set from a repository-wide claim/reference scan and explicitly add every surviving hit to the file list, including the retention/protection policy, dependency/infrastructure policy, opportunity-cost document, and the stale OAuth module comment. Classify historical review records as intentional immutable references rather than trying to erase their history.
findings.win: Makes the scope gate internally consistent and prevents shipping mutually contradictory security, retention, infrastructure, and identity documentation.

findings.title: The teardown leaves the acknowledged deauthorization obligation behind
findings.claim: The sketch retains the published-app OAuth installation path while explicitly stating that Zoom still requires a deauthorization endpoint, then treats that endpoint as an unrelated pre-existing gap. Eliminating stored rate data changes the webhook’s deletion work but does not, by the sketch’s own premise, eliminate the marketplace endpoint obligation. Removing the reusable identity/purge path and publishing revised compliance claims without a replacement plan makes that obligation easier to overlook and potentially harder to satisfy later.
findings.alternative: Separate data deletion from protocol compliance: either include a minimal Zoom deauthorization/data-compliance endpoint that validates the event and returns the required confirmation with a no-op presenter-data purge, or make deployment/marketplace submission explicitly gated on a follow-on story with an owner and acceptance test. Preserve only the minimal verification primitives that endpoint actually needs; do not retain `resolveUid` merely by default.
findings.win: Closes or explicitly gates a known marketplace blocker without preserving the obsolete rate-store architecture.
